questions.explanation: Density rho = m / V (kg/m^3) — mass per unit volume. Water: rho = 1000 kg/m^3. Air at sea level: rho = 1.225 kg/m^3. Mercury: rho = 13,600 kg/m^3 — high density supports the classic mercury barometer (Torricelli, 1643). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain density with buoyancy (F_b = rho_fluid V_displaced g) and pressure (P = rho g h). Density depends on temperature (water expands when heated, becoming less dense — driving thermal convection and ocean currents) and pressure (negligible for liquids in AP problems, large for gases — see ideal gas law rho = PM/RT in Unit 2 thermodynamics).

Pressure P = F / A (Pa = N/m^2), force exerted perpendicular to a surface per unit contact area. Blaise Pascal (1647) systematized pressure in static fluids and gave name to the SI unit. Pressure is scalar in static fluids — acts equally in all directions at a point (Pascal's principle). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute pressure at depth (P = P_atm + rho g h), pressure on dam walls (P varies linearly with depth, so integrate), and pressure in pneumatic systems. Connects to gas behavior in Unit 2 via ideal gas law PV = nRT. Atmospheric pressure: 1 atm = 1.013 * 10^5 Pa = 760 mmHg = 14.7 psi.

Pascal (Pa) = 1 N/m^2 = 1 kg / (m * s^2). Named after Blaise Pascal (1623-1662), who established fluid pressure principles and invented the early barometer experiments demonstrating atmospheric pressure varies with altitude. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: P = rho g h with rho in kg/m^3, g in m/s^2, h in m gives Pa automatically. Practical magnitudes: atmospheric pressure ~10^5 Pa; tire pressure ~2 * 10^5 Pa gauge (~3 * 10^5 absolute); blood pressure ~10^4 Pa gauge (~100 mmHg systolic). Vacuum systems: 1 Torr = 133 Pa. 1 atm = 101,325 Pa.

Hydrostatic pressure: P(h) = P_0 + rho g h (Pa), where h is depth, rho is fluid density, g is gravitational acceleration, P_0 is surface pressure (typically atmospheric ~10^5 Pa). Pascal's hydrostatic equation. On the AP equation sheet. Linear in depth. Derived from F = m g of a fluid column over area A: P = m g / A = rho V g / A = rho A h g / A = rho g h. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: at 10 m in water, P_gauge = (1000)(9.8)(10) ≈ 10^5 Pa ≈ 1 atm. Submarines at 300 m face ~30 atm. Independent of container shape — Pascal's vases paradox.

questions.prompt: Pressure increase with depth in incompressible fluid.
questions.explanation: In an incompressible fluid (typical liquid), pressure increases LINEARLY with depth: P(h) = P_0 + rho g h. The slope dP/dh = rho g is constant — for water, dP/dh = (1000)(9.8) = 9800 Pa/m, so every 10 m of water adds ~1 atm. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) plot P vs h to extract rho or g from the slope. Compressible fluids (gases): dP/dh = -rho(P) g, an exponential decay (atmospheric pressure halves every ~5500 m altitude). Linear approximation valid for liquids because compressibility is negligible — water density changes < 0.5% over 1 km depth.

Buoyancy F_b is the upward force on any submerged object due to the pressure difference between its deeper (high-P) and shallower (low-P) surfaces. Magnitude given by Archimedes' principle: F_b = rho_fluid V_displaced g (newtons). Discovered by Archimedes (c. 250 BCE) when testing whether King Hieron's crown was pure gold — the apocryphal 'Eureka!' moment. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain buoyancy with weight for floating/sinking analysis: F_b > F_g → accelerates upward; F_b < F_g → sinks; F_b = F_g → floats at equilibrium with V_displaced / V_object = rho_object / rho_fluid.

Archimedes' principle: the buoyant force equals the weight of the fluid displaced. F_b = rho_fluid V_disp g (newtons). Established by Archimedes c. 250 BCE. On the AP equation sheet. Independent of the object's density — depends only on fluid density and displaced volume. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic: a steel ship floats because the hull encloses a large volume, achieving F_b >= F_g; a solid steel cube of the same mass sinks because V_disp is too small. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) about submarines: ballast tanks flood with water to increase rho_avg above rho_water → sink; expel water to rise.

A floating object is in equilibrium: F_b = F_g. Setting rho_fluid V_disp g = m g = rho_obj V_obj g gives V_disp / V_obj = rho_obj / rho_fluid — the fraction submerged equals the density ratio. Iceberg in water: rho_ice / rho_water ≈ 0.917, so ~92% submerged (the proverbial 'tip of the iceberg'). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute submerged fractions for various density combinations: wood (rho ~700) floats with 70% submerged in water; cork (rho ~250) with 25%; person in saltwater (rho ~1027) floats slightly higher than in fresh water (rho 1000).

Specific gravity (SG) = rho_substance / rho_water (dimensionless, where rho_water = 1000 kg/m^3 at 4 °C). SG > 1 means the substance is denser than water (sinks); SG < 1 means it floats. Convenient for floating/sinking analyses: a substance with SG = 0.85 floats with 85% submerged. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) use SG in hydrometer problems and oil-water mixture problems. Common SG values: ice 0.917, cork 0.25, wood 0.4-0.9, aluminum 2.7, iron 7.87, gold 19.3, mercury 13.6. Hydrometers (Galileo's invention) measure SG directly by floating depth.

questions.prompt: Object denser than fluid does this.
questions.explanation: An object with average density greater than the fluid sinks: rho_obj > rho_fluid means F_b < F_g even when fully submerged, so net force is downward. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: a steel ball (rho = 7850 kg/m^3) in water (1000) experiences F_b = rho_water V g and F_g = rho_steel V g, with F_g 7.85x larger — sinks readily. Net downward force = (rho_obj - rho_fluid) V g; net downward acceleration a = g(1 - rho_fluid/rho_obj). At terminal velocity (in a viscous fluid), drag balances net force; AP-level often neglects drag. Submarines flooding ballast tanks: rho_avg > rho_water → controlled descent.

Pascal's principle (1647): in a confined incompressible fluid at rest, pressure applied at one point is transmitted undiminished to every other point. Blaise Pascal demonstrated this experimentally with hydraulic apparatus. Foundation of all hydraulic machinery. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use Pascal's principle in hydraulic-press problems: a small piston (area A_1) under force F_1 produces pressure P = F_1/A_1, transmitted through the fluid to a large piston (area A_2 >> A_1) where lifting force is F_2 = P A_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1). Mechanical advantage A_2/A_1 explains how a person can lift a car with a hydraulic jack.

Hydraulic press: two connected fluid-filled chambers with pistons of different areas. Small force F_1 on small piston (A_1) creates pressure P = F_1/A_1, transmitted (Pascal's principle) to large piston (A_2). Output force F_2 = P A_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1) — mechanical advantage A_2/A_1. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a car-jack hydraulic with A_2/A_1 = 100 lifts a 1000 kg car with only ~98 N input force. Volume conservation: V_1 = V_2 → A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2, so small piston moves 100x further. Energy conserved: F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2. Applications: car brakes, dental chairs, garbage trucks, construction equipment.

Mechanical advantage in a hydraulic press = A_2 / A_1 (output area / input area). Force amplification F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a 200:1 area ratio lifts a 20,000 N car with only 100 N input. The cost (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): the small piston must travel 200x farther — energy is conserved, since F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2 → both sides represent work W. Hydraulics multiply force at the expense of distance, analogous to mechanical levers (Archimedes' lever principle). Used in car jacks, brake systems, construction equipment, dental chairs.

Volume is conserved in a hydraulic system because the working fluid (oil, water) is incompressible: V_1 = V_2 → A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2, where d_1, d_2 are piston displacements. Combined with Pascal's principle F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1, this yields F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2 = W (work conserved). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test the trade-off: a 100x force amplification requires 100x distance reduction at the output piston. Energy conservation is intact: input work equals output work (neglecting friction in the system). This is mechanical advantage at the cost of distance — same principle as Archimedes' lever.

questions.prompt: Smaller piston in hydraulics moves how.
questions.explanation: In a hydraulic system, the smaller (input) piston travels a longer distance than the larger (output) piston, in inverse proportion to area: d_1 / d_2 = A_2 / A_1. For a 100:1 area ratio, the input piston moves 100x farther while exerting 100x less force, with energy conservation F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2 = W. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test in paragraph-length arguments: students must explain why hydraulic force amplification is NOT a violation of energy conservation — the trade-off is distance. Same trade-off as long-lever mechanical advantage (Archimedes), simple machines, and gear ratios.

questions.prompt: Equation conserving mass in steady flow.
questions.explanation: Continuity equation: A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 (m^3/s), volume flow rate constant for incompressible steady flow through a tube. Expresses conservation of mass: whatever enters at section 1 must exit at section 2. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply this to garden hoses (nozzle narrows, water speed increases), river constrictions (narrow gorge = faster current), blood-vessel narrowings (atherosclerosis increases blood velocity, lowering pressure by Bernoulli — explains aneurysm formation). For compressible flow: rho_1 A_1 v_1 = rho_2 A_2 v_2 (mass flow rate constant).

choices: Eight point nine nine times ten to the ninth
questions.explanation: Volume flow rate Q = A v (m^3/s) — cross-sectional area times average flow speed. Conserved in incompressible steady flow (continuity). On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): water flowing at 2 m/s through a pipe of cross-section 0.01 m^2 has Q = 0.02 m^3/s = 20 L/s. Filling a 100 L tank takes 5 s. Mass flow rate: m_dot = rho Q (kg/s). Total volume that flowed through a section in time t: V = Q t. Used in plumbing design, irrigation, and circulatory-system blood-flow analyses (Q_blood ≈ 5 L/min through the heart at rest).

When an incompressible fluid flows through a narrowing pipe (A decreases), velocity INCREASES proportionally: v_2 = v_1 (A_1/A_2). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): if A halves, v doubles. Combined with Bernoulli's equation, narrowing also DECREASES pressure (P_2 < P_1) — the basis for venturi meters, carburetors, and aspirators. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7): students explain why a garden hose with thumb partially over the nozzle shoots water farther — narrowing increases v (by continuity), which by Bernoulli also lowers pressure inside the constriction (sucking adjacent air in for atomization).

The simple continuity equation A v = constant assumes INCOMPRESSIBLE flow (constant density rho). Valid for liquids at AP-level (water density varies < 0.5% per km depth) and for gases at speeds well below the speed of sound (Mach < 0.3). For compressible flow (high-speed gas, supersonic jets), the more general rho A v = constant applies — and density itself changes with pressure. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to declare incompressibility before applying simple continuity. Real liquid flow is approximately incompressible for typical AP scenarios — pipes, garden hoses, blood vessels, airfoils at subsonic speeds.

Mass flow rate m_dot = rho A v (kg/s) — density times area times velocity. Conserved in steady flow (incompressible OR compressible): rho_1 A_1 v_1 = rho_2 A_2 v_2. For incompressible flow (rho constant), reduces to A v = constant. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) chain mass flow rate with energy: kinetic energy flow rate = (1/2) m_dot v^2 = (1/2) rho A v^3 (watts) — used in wind turbine power analyses, where extractable power scales with v^3 (cube law). Rocket-propulsion thrust: F = m_dot * v_exhaust — Tsiolkovsky's rocket equation built on this.

choices: n_1 sin theta_1 equals n_2 sin theta_2
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's equation: P + (1/2) rho v^2 + rho g h = constant along a streamline (Pa) for incompressible inviscid steady flow. Daniel Bernoulli (1738, 'Hydrodynamica') derived it from energy conservation applied to a fluid element. On the AP equation sheet. Three forms of energy density: pressure (P), kinetic ((1/2) rho v^2), gravitational (rho g h). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Bernoulli with continuity (A v = constant) to solve for pressure differences in pipes, airplane lift, blood-flow pressure, Torricelli's tank-drainage law. Implicit: steady, inviscid, incompressible — students must declare assumptions.

Bernoulli effect: pressure is LOWER where fluid speed is HIGHER (along the same streamline at the same height). Direct consequence of Bernoulli's equation P + (1/2) rho v^2 = constant (for horizontal flow). Daniel Bernoulli (1738). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic applications: airfoils generate lift because air moves faster over the curved top than the flatter bottom, creating lower pressure above; spinning baseballs curve (Magnus effect — surface drags air asymmetrically); vacuum-cleaner nozzle reduces pressure for suction; venturi meters measure flow rate by pressure drop in constrictions; aspirators atomize fluid into airstreams.

Airfoil lift: air moves faster over the curved upper wing surface than the flatter lower surface (continuity + airfoil shape), so by the Bernoulli effect the upper pressure is LOWER than the lower pressure, creating a net upward force = (P_lower - P_upper) * Wing_area. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require explicit invocation of both continuity (which sets the speed difference) and Bernoulli (which relates speed to pressure). Note: simple Bernoulli explanation is incomplete in detail — full lift theory needs the Kutta condition and circulation theory (Joukowski 1906) — but AP stays at the Bernoulli level. Lift balances weight in level flight; exceeds weight in climb.

Bernoulli's equation assumes: (1) incompressible flow (constant rho), (2) inviscid (no viscous dissipation), (3) steady (no time-dependent flow patterns), (4) along a streamline (different streamlines may have different Bernoulli constants). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to declare these assumptions before applying Bernoulli. Real flows violate at least viscosity (turbulent pipe flow loses energy to friction); compressibility (subsonic gas flow approximately incompressible only at low Mach). For high-speed gas, drag, or viscous flows, more sophisticated models (Navier-Stokes equations) apply — beyond AP.

Total head: H = P/(rho g) + v^2/(2g) + h (meters), the Bernoulli equation divided by rho g. Three components: pressure head (P/(rho g)), velocity head (v^2/(2g)), and elevation head (h). Conserved along a streamline in ideal flow. Used in civil engineering (water supply, dam design) and hydraulic engineering. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) chain total head in tank-drainage problems: a tank of height h drains through a small hole at the bottom with v = sqrt(2 g h) — Torricelli's law (1644), derived by setting P_top = P_bottom = atm and v_top ≈ 0. Same form as free-fall: v_drain = v_free-fall from height h.

choices: Eight point nine nine times ten to the ninth
questions.explanation: Temperature T (Kelvin) is proportional to the average translational kinetic energy of particles: KE_avg = (3/2) k_B T, where k_B = 1.38 * 10^-23 J/K is Boltzmann's constant. Maxwell (1860) and Boltzmann (1870s) developed kinetic theory linking macroscopic T to microscopic motion. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in ideal-gas problems and in rms-speed calculations: v_rms = sqrt(3 k_B T / m). T is a scalar — not vector, even though particles have random directional motion. Absolute zero (T = 0 K) corresponds to minimum particle KE (residual zero-point energy from quantum mechanics, beyond AP-level).

Kelvin (K) — the SI absolute temperature scale, with zero at absolute zero and 1 K interval equal to 1 °C interval. Named after William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin (1824-1907). Conversion: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15. Water freezes at 273.15 K (0 °C), boils at 373.15 K (100 °C). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) REQUIRE Kelvin in ideal-gas law PV = nRT and in entropy/heat-engine efficiency formulas — Celsius gives wrong answers because temperature ratios are needed. Boltzmann's constant k_B = 1.38 * 10^-23 J/K links T (in Kelvin) to particle KE.

choices: One over f equals 1 over d_o plus 1 over d_i
questions.explanation: Absolute zero: T = 0 K = -273.15 °C = -459.67 °F. The lowest theoretically achievable temperature, where classical particle KE → 0. Predicted by Guillaume Amontons (~1700), formalized by Kelvin (1848). The Third Law of Thermodynamics (Nernst, 1906) states absolute zero is unattainable in finite steps. Modern laboratories reach ~10^-12 K with laser cooling and Bose-Einstein condensate techniques (Cornell-Wieman, 2001 Nobel). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test conceptual understanding: at T = 0 K, ideal gas P → 0 and V → 0 (per PV = nRT extrapolated); real gases liquefy or solidify before reaching 0 K.

Heat Q (joules) is energy transferred from a higher-temperature body to a lower-temperature one due to the temperature difference. Spontaneous flow direction (Second Law of Thermodynamics, Clausius 1850). NOT the same as temperature — heat is energy transferred; temperature measures average KE. Joule (1840s) established the mechanical equivalent: 1 cal = 4.184 J. Three modes: conduction (direct contact), convection (fluid motion), radiation (EM waves). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use Q = m c Delta T for specific-heat problems, Q = mL for phase changes, and Q in the First Law: Delta U = Q - W.

Specific heat capacity c unit: J/(kg * K). Q = m c Delta T (joules), where m is mass (kg), Delta T is temperature change (K or °C — intervals are identical), and c is specific heat. Water: c = 4186 J/(kg*K) (high — explains water's heat-storage role in climate moderation). Aluminum: 900 J/(kg*K). Copper: 387 J/(kg*K). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 4: Experimental Design) often ask students to measure c by calorimetry: place a known mass at known initial T into water, observe equilibrium T, and apply Q_in = Q_out. Black (1761) discovered specific heat capacity, distinguishing it from temperature.

questions.prompt: Equation relating P, V, n, T.
questions.explanation: Ideal gas law: PV = nRT (Pa * m^3 = J), where P is pressure (Pa), V volume (m^3), n moles, R = 8.314 J/(mol*K) the universal gas constant, T temperature (K, MUST be absolute). On the AP equation sheet. Synthesizes Boyle's law (P V = const at constant T), Charles's law (V/T = const at constant P), and Avogadro's law (V/n = const at constant P, T). Maxwell-Boltzmann kinetic theory (1860s-70s) derived PV = nRT from first principles. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this for isothermal, isobaric, and isochoric processes. Real gases deviate near condensation (van der Waals).

questions.prompt: Constant R in ideal gas law.
questions.explanation: Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/(mol*K) = 0.0821 L*atm/(mol*K). Appears in PV = nRT (ideal gas law) and Delta U = (3/2) n R T (internal energy of monatomic ideal gas). Connects molar quantities (n in moles) to energy. Related to Boltzmann's constant k_B by R = N_A k_B, where N_A = 6.022 * 10^23 /mol is Avogadro's number. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: J units give SI Kelvin; L*atm version useful for chemistry-style problems. Universal — same R for all gases, an empirical foundation of the ideal-gas model. Identical for helium, oxygen, or CO2 (in ideal limit).

Isobaric process: pressure constant (Delta P = 0) while V and T change. Charles's law (1787): V/T = constant. Work done by gas: W_by_gas = P Delta V (J) — area under horizontal line on PV diagram. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain isobaric processes with the First Law: Q = Delta U + W. For monatomic ideal gas: Delta U = (3/2) n R Delta T and W = P Delta V = n R Delta T (since PV = nRT and Delta P = 0), so Q = (5/2) n R Delta T → C_P = (5/2) R for monatomic. Examples: gas in a frictionless piston with atmospheric pressure on top; heating a gas in an open balloon.

Isochoric process: volume constant (Delta V = 0) while P and T change. Also called isovolumetric or isometric. Gay-Lussac's law: P/T = constant. Work done by gas: W = P Delta V = 0 (no volume change, no work). First Law reduces to Q = Delta U: all heat goes to internal energy. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: for monatomic ideal gas, Q = Delta U = (3/2) n R Delta T → C_V = (3/2) R per mole. Mayer's relation: C_P - C_V = R. PV diagram shows isochoric as vertical line. Examples: heating gas in a rigid sealed container; combustion in an idealized constant-volume bomb calorimeter.

Isothermal process: temperature constant (Delta T = 0) while P and V change. Boyle's law (1662): PV = constant. For ideal gas: Delta U = 0 (internal energy depends only on T), so First Law gives Q = W — all heat absorbed becomes work done by gas. Work: W = n R T ln(V_f / V_i) (J). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain isothermal expansion: gas absorbs heat from a reservoir, does positive work, and stays at constant T (requires good thermal contact with reservoir). PV diagram: isothermal is a hyperbola (P proportional to 1/V). Carnot used isothermal expansion in his idealized engine cycle.

choices: Delta U equals Q minus W
questions.explanation: First Law of Thermodynamics: Delta U = Q - W (joules), where Delta U is change in internal energy, Q is heat added to the system, W is work done BY the system on surroundings. Conservation of energy applied to thermodynamic systems. Established by Julius Mayer (1842), Joule (paddle-wheel experiments 1840s), Helmholtz (1847), and Clausius. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with PV-diagram analyses: Q, W, Delta U must be tracked separately for each leg of a cycle, and a Carnot cycle returns to start with Delta U = 0 overall but Q_net = W_net (engine output).

choices: I equals I_0 cosine squared theta
Delta U equals Q minus W
questions.correctText: Delta U equals Q minus W
questions.explanation: Delta U = Q - W (joules) — First Law of Thermodynamics. Sign conventions (AP standard): Q > 0 when heat is ADDED to the system; W > 0 when work is done BY the system on surroundings. (Some textbooks use Delta U = Q + W with W = work done ON system — same physics, opposite sign convention.) On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require careful sign tracking. For closed cycles: Delta U = 0 over one full cycle (state function), so Q_net = W_net — heat absorbed equals net work output. Limiting cases: isochoric → W = 0 → Q = Delta U; isothermal ideal gas → Delta U = 0 → Q = W.

questions.prompt: Internal energy of monatomic ideal gas.
choices: Delta U equals Q minus W
questions.explanation: Monatomic ideal gas internal energy: U = (3/2) n R T (joules). Each atom has 3 translational degrees of freedom, each contributing (1/2) k_B T per atom (equipartition theorem) → (3/2) k_B T per atom → (3/2) N k_B T = (3/2) n R T per n moles. Diatomic gas (like O_2, N_2): U = (5/2) n R T at moderate T (3 translational + 2 rotational), reaching (7/2) n R T at high T when vibrational modes activate. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Delta U = (3/2) n R Delta T into the First Law: Q = Delta U + W. Examples: helium and argon are monatomic (noble gases).

questions.prompt: Work done by gas in expansion.
questions.explanation: When a gas expands (Delta V > 0) against external pressure, the gas does POSITIVE work on surroundings: W = integral P dV > 0 (joules). For constant P (isobaric): W = P Delta V > 0. For isothermal expansion of ideal gas: W = n R T ln(V_f/V_i) > 0 since V_f > V_i. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require sign awareness: expansion → +W (gas does work on surroundings, like pushing a piston outward); compression → -W (surroundings do work on gas, like pushing piston inward). Useful PV-diagram fact: W_by_gas = area under the P-V curve (positive when moving right, negative when moving left).

questions.prompt: Heat added to gas means this.
choices: Delta U equals Q minus W
questions.explanation: Heat ADDED to a system corresponds to Q > 0 (AP sign convention). Heat REMOVED (system loses heat) corresponds to Q < 0. By the First Law Delta U = Q - W, adding heat with no work done (isochoric process, W = 0) raises internal energy by exactly Q: Delta U = Q. For isothermal expansion: Delta U = 0 (T constant), so all added Q becomes work W = Q > 0. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require students to track Q for each leg of a cycle. Heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold (Second Law). Engines use Q_h > 0 from hot reservoir, Q_c < 0 to cold reservoir (heat dumped).

Entropy S (J/K) — a measure of disorder or the number of microstates corresponding to a given macrostate. Clausius (1865) coined the term; Boltzmann (1877) gave the statistical definition S = k_B ln(W), where W is the number of microstates. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) use entropy qualitatively at the AP level: a gas spreading to fill a container has higher entropy than gas confined to a corner; mixing fluids increases entropy; heating an object increases entropy. Second Law (Clausius): in any isolated process, total entropy of universe non-decreasing (Delta S_universe >= 0). Reversible processes: Delta S_universe = 0; irreversible: Delta S_universe > 0.

Second Law of Thermodynamics: total entropy of an isolated system (or the universe) never decreases — increases for irreversible processes, stays constant for reversible. Clausius (1865): 'Heat does not spontaneously flow from cold to hot.' Kelvin-Planck (1851): 'No engine can convert heat completely to work in a cycle.' AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test conceptual implications: heat flows spontaneously hot to cold; the universe trends toward thermal equilibrium (heat death); no engine can be 100% efficient. Boltzmann (1877): S = k_B ln(W) connects macroscopic entropy to microstate count — explains why disorder dominates statistically.

Carnot engine: an idealized heat engine running on the Carnot cycle (two isothermals + two adiabats) between two thermal reservoirs. Achieves maximum efficiency: eta_max = 1 - T_c/T_h (with T in Kelvin). Sadi Carnot (1824, 'Reflections on the Motive Power of Fire') established this upper bound BEFORE the Second Law was formalized — a stunning early thermodynamic insight. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use eta = 1 - T_c/T_h to set efficiency ceilings: a real engine with T_h = 600 K, T_c = 300 K has at most eta = 50%; real engines achieve ~30-40% due to friction, finite-time effects, and non-Carnot cycles.

Heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold — Clausius's statement of the Second Law (1850). Reverse flow (cold to hot) requires work input (e.g., refrigerators, heat pumps). The flow direction follows from entropy increase: removing heat Q from hot (T_h) decreases its S by Q/T_h; adding to cold (T_c) increases S by Q/T_c. Since T_c < T_h, the increase exceeds the decrease, so Delta S_total > 0 — the process is spontaneous. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this in paragraph-length arguments about refrigerators (which DO move heat cold → hot but consume work to do so) and air conditioners (same principle).

Heat engine efficiency: eta = W_net / Q_h, where W_net is net work output per cycle and Q_h is heat absorbed from the hot reservoir. Dimensionless, ranges 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%). By the First Law: Q_h = W_net + Q_c, so eta = 1 - Q_c/Q_h. Carnot ceiling: eta_max = 1 - T_c/T_h. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: a power plant with T_h = 800 K, T_c = 300 K has Carnot ceiling eta_max = 62.5%; real plants achieve ~40% due to non-Carnot cycles. Refrigerator COP = Q_c / W (heat removed per work input) — different metric for inverse operation.

PV diagram: a plot of pressure (vertical axis) vs volume (horizontal axis) for a thermodynamic process. Each point represents an equilibrium state of the gas (P, V), with T determined by ideal-gas law T = PV/(nR). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) read off Q, W, Delta U for each leg by recognizing process types: horizontal line = isobaric, vertical = isochoric, hyperbola = isothermal, steeper-than-hyperbola = adiabatic. Area under curve = work done by gas. Closed cycles: clockwise → net positive work (engine); counterclockwise → net negative work (refrigerator/heat pump).

Work done BY the gas on surroundings: W = integral P dV (joules) — the area under the PV curve. Sign convention: rightward motion (V increasing, expansion) gives positive W; leftward (compression) gives negative W. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) compute W by counting graph paper squares or applying geometric formulas: rectangle area for isobaric (W = P Delta V); triangle area for linear P(V); hyperbola integration for isothermal (W = n R T ln(V_f/V_i)). For isochoric, area = 0. Closed cycle: net W = enclosed area, positive for CW, negative for CCW.

A closed cycle on a PV diagram returns to the initial state — same P, V, T. Internal energy U is a state function, so Delta U_cycle = 0 over one full cycle. First Law: Q_net = W_net. The net work over a cycle equals the ENCLOSED AREA on the PV diagram. Sign: clockwise traversal (heat engine cycle) → W_net > 0 (engine produces work); counterclockwise (refrigerator cycle) → W_net < 0 (work input required, heat moves from cold to hot). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) compute enclosed area for various cycle shapes (Carnot rectangular on T-S diagram, hourglass on PV).

A clockwise (CW) traversal of a closed PV cycle yields POSITIVE net work output — this is a heat engine cycle. The gas absorbs heat at high T, expands (doing positive work on surroundings), rejects waste heat at low T, then is compressed back to start (less work input than was extracted, because compression occurs at lower P than expansion). Net W = enclosed area > 0. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) classify cycle types by direction: CW = engine (steam turbines, internal combustion, Carnot). CCW = refrigerator (consume work to move heat against gradient). Efficiency eta = W_net / Q_h, bounded above by Carnot (1 - T_c/T_h).

A counter-clockwise (CCW) traversal of a closed PV cycle yields NEGATIVE net work — net work is INPUT to the system (refrigerator or heat pump cycle). The gas is compressed at high T, releases heat to the hot reservoir, expands (cooled), and absorbs heat from the cold reservoir. Net W = -enclosed area < 0 (work is consumed). The Second Law forbids spontaneous cold→hot heat flow, but the work input enables it (Clausius statement: 'no process whose sole effect is to move heat cold to hot' — work expenditure is the additional effect). Refrigerator COP = Q_c / |W|; heat pump COP = Q_h / |W|.

choices: Eight point nine nine times ten to the ninth
k q_1 q_2 over r squared
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law (Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, 1785): the electrostatic force between two point charges has magnitude F = k |q_1 q_2| / r^2 (newtons), directed along the line joining them — repulsive for like charges, attractive for unlike. Coulomb's constant k = 8.99 * 10^9 N*m^2/C^2. On the AP equation sheet. Inverse-square law analogous to Newton's gravity but ~10^39 times stronger. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Coulomb's law with vector addition for multi-charge systems (superposition principle): F_net = sum of pairwise Coulomb forces. Coulomb measured it with a torsion balance similar to Cavendish's gravity setup.

choices: k q_1 q_2 over r squared
Eight point nine nine times ten to the ninth
questions.correctText: k q_1 q_2 over r squared
questions.explanation: F = k q_1 q_2 / r^2 (N), where k = 8.99 * 10^9 N*m^2/C^2, q_1 and q_2 are charges (C), r is separation (m). Sign: positive product → repulsive force; negative product → attractive. On the AP equation sheet. Equivalent form: F = q_1 q_2 / (4 pi epsilon_0 r^2), where epsilon_0 = 8.85 * 10^-12 C^2/(N*m^2) is the permittivity of free space; k = 1/(4 pi epsilon_0). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test inverse-square scaling: doubling r reduces F by factor 4; tripling reduces by 9. Compare to gravity: m_1 m_2 G / r^2 (also inverse-square), but Coulomb forces dominate electromagnetic interactions at atomic scale.

choices: Eight point nine nine times ten to the ninth
questions.correctText: Eight point nine nine times ten to the ninth
questions.explanation: Coulomb's constant k = 8.99 * 10^9 N*m^2/C^2 (often approximated 9 * 10^9 on AP). Connects q_1, q_2 (C) and r (m) to F (N) in Coulomb's law. Related to vacuum permittivity: k = 1/(4 pi epsilon_0), with epsilon_0 = 8.85 * 10^-12 C^2/(N*m^2). On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require this value in numerical computations: two 1 C charges 1 m apart experience F = 9 * 10^9 N — a colossal force, illustrating why net charges in normal matter are tiny (usually < 10^-6 C). Defines the strength of electrostatic interaction relative to gravity (10^39x stronger for proton-proton).

Coulomb (C) — SI unit of electric charge. 1 C = current of 1 A flowing for 1 s. Named after Charles-Augustin de Coulomb. 1 C = ~6.24 * 10^18 elementary charges (electrons or protons). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: q in C, r in m, k in N*m^2/C^2 gives F in N. Practical magnitudes: a lightning bolt transfers ~15 C of charge; a fully charged capacitor in a typical AP problem holds ~10^-6 C (microcoulomb); the electron has q_e = -1.6 * 10^-19 C. Total charge on a person scuffing carpet might reach ~10^-7 C (static shock).

choices: k q_1 q_2 over r squared
questions.explanation: Electron charge q_e = -1.602 * 10^-19 C (often approximated -1.6 * 10^-19 on AP). Magnitude e = 1.6 * 10^-19 C — the elementary charge unit. Robert Millikan (1909) measured this precisely with his oil-drop experiment (Nobel 1923). Proton charge: +e. All observed charges are integer multiples of e (charge quantization) — even quarks (charge ±1/3 e or ±2/3 e) are confined inside hadrons and never seen free. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) chain e with photon energy in the photoelectric effect (Unit 7): KE_max = eV_stop, where V_stop is stopping voltage and KE in electron-volts (1 eV = 1.6 * 10^-19 J).

Electric field E = F / q_test (N/C or V/m) — force per unit test charge. Vector pointing from + to - (away from positive source charges). Faraday (1830s) introduced the field concept to explain action-at-a-distance — fields fill space and exert forces on charges placed in them. Maxwell formalized the field theory (1865). On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute E from source charges (E = k Q / r^2 for point), then F = q E for test charge in the field. Superposition: E_net from multiple sources = vector sum of individual contributions. Field lines: arrows point along E, density indicates magnitude.

Electric field unit: N/C (newtons per coulomb), equivalent to V/m (volts per meter). E in V/m clarifies the relation to electric potential: E = -dV/dr. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: q in C and E in N/C gives F = qE in N. Practical magnitudes: Earth's atmospheric E ~ 100 V/m (fair-weather field); thunderstorm fields ~ 10^4 to 10^5 V/m; lightning breakdown at ~3 * 10^6 V/m in dry air; inside a hydrogen atom at the Bohr radius ~ 5 * 10^11 V/m (huge — explains why atomic-scale interactions are dominantly electromagnetic, not gravitational).

Electric field of a point charge: E = k Q / r^2 (N/C), magnitude only, directed radially outward for Q > 0 (inward for Q < 0). Direct from Coulomb's law: E = F/q = (k Q q / r^2) / q. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute E at a point due to multiple charges by vector superposition. Inverse-square law mirrors gravity: g = G M / r^2. Limiting cases (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): r → 0 gives E → infinity (idealized point-charge singularity); r → infinity gives E → 0. Real charge distributions: integrate dE = k dq/r^2 over the source — beyond AP level usually.

Electric field points AWAY from positive charges and TOWARD negative charges. Convention defined by force on a positive test charge — if test charge q > 0, F = qE is in the direction of E. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require explicit drawing of field lines with arrows: out of positive, into negative. Field-line density indicates magnitude (denser = stronger E). Field lines: never cross (E is single-valued at each point), perpendicular to conductor surfaces (charges rearrange on conductors to make tangential E zero), and start/end on charges (unless extending to infinity). Faraday introduced these visualization tools in the 1830s.

Inside an ideal conductor in electrostatic equilibrium, E = 0 everywhere. Reason (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): free electrons rearrange instantly under any applied field, redistributing surface charges until internal E cancels out. Equivalent: equipotential interior (V = constant), since E = -dV/dr = 0 implies V is uniform. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6) test this in Faraday-cage scenarios: a hollow conductor shields its interior from external fields (Faraday's ice-pail experiment, 1843). Practical applications: shielded coaxial cables, MRI rooms, EMP-protective enclosures. Charges on the conductor reside on the surface; any excess charge migrates outward immediately.

Electric potential V = U/q (volts = J/C) — electric potential energy per unit charge. Scalar quantity. Difference Delta V between two points = work done per unit charge moving from one to the other (work done by external agent against the electric field, OR negative of work done by field). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain V with E via V = -integral E dr (or Delta V = -E * d for uniform field). Used in capacitors (Q = CV), circuits (Ohm's V = IR), and energy conservation problems (energy change of charge q moving through Delta V is q Delta V). On the AP equation sheet. Reference point arbitrary.

Volt (V) = 1 J/C — SI unit of electric potential. 1 V means 1 J of energy per coulomb of charge. Named after Alessandro Volta (1745-1827), inventor of the voltaic pile (first chemical battery, 1800). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: Delta V in V, q in C → energy q Delta V in J. Practical magnitudes: typical AA battery 1.5 V; car battery 12 V; household outlet 120 V (USA) / 230 V (Europe); power-line transmission 100s of kV; lightning discharge ~10^8 V. Inside a typical AP-style circuit problem with 9 V battery moving 1 C, the battery delivers 9 J of energy to the circuit.

Electric potential of a point charge: V = k Q / r (volts), with reference V = 0 at infinity. Falls off as 1/r (slower than E which falls as 1/r^2). Sign: positive for Q > 0, negative for Q < 0. On the AP equation sheet. Derived by V = -integral E dr from infinity to r. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this with the superposition principle: V at a point due to multiple charges is the SCALAR sum (much easier than vector sum needed for E). Then U_int = q_test V at that point gives potential energy of placing q_test there. Multi-charge total PE: U = sum over pairs of k q_i q_j / r_ij.

Electric field is the negative gradient of potential: E = -dV/dr in 1D (or E = -grad V in 3D). E (V/m) and V (V) are related by spatial derivative. For uniform field between parallel plates: E = V / d (V/m), where d is plate separation. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain E and V: in a capacitor with V = 12 V across d = 1 mm, E = 12,000 V/m = 12 kV/m, pointing from + to - plate. The sign indicates direction: E points from high V to low V (downhill in potential, like gravity pulling masses downhill in gravitational PE). Equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to E.

Equipotential surface: a set of points all at the same potential V — work done moving a charge along it is ZERO (W = q Delta V = 0). Always perpendicular to electric field lines (E points along steepest V decrease). Conductors in electrostatic equilibrium have their entire volume at the same potential (equipotential body), and surface charges arrange to make E perpendicular to surface (no tangential E, since tangential E would push charges along surface, violating equilibrium). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) use equipotential lines as the V-analog of contour lines on a topographic map. Close-spaced equipotentials → strong E (steep potential gradient).

A conductor is a material with free charge carriers (electrons in metals, ions in electrolytes) that move readily under electric fields. Examples: copper, aluminum, gold, silver, graphite, salt water. Drude (1900) modeled conduction as electrons moving freely between atomic collisions. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) treat conductors as: (1) interior E = 0 in equilibrium; (2) entire body equipotential; (3) excess charge resides on surface; (4) E perpendicular to surface. Resistance: rho_resistivity for copper ~ 1.7 * 10^-8 Ohm*m. Silver is the best metallic conductor (slightly better than copper), but copper is used in wiring for cost.

An insulator (dielectric) is a material with no free charge carriers — electrons tightly bound to atoms. Examples: glass, rubber, plastic, dry wood, air. High resistivity rho > 10^14 Ohm*m for typical insulators (vs ~10^-8 for copper). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) treat insulators as: (1) can be polarized (atomic dipoles align in external E, reducing net E inside); (2) hold electric charge on the surface where deposited; (3) used between capacitor plates to increase capacitance by dielectric constant kappa. Dielectric breakdown: applying field > breakdown limit (~3 * 10^6 V/m for air) ionizes the insulator (lightning, electrical arcing).

Charging by induction: a neutral conductor brought near a charged object has its free charges polarize — opposite charges migrate toward the nearby charge, like charges migrate away. Grounding the far side while the inducing charge is still near, then removing both the ground and the inducing charge, leaves the conductor with a net charge opposite to the inducer. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test the sequence in paragraph-length arguments: students must explain the order of operations and which side becomes charged. Contrast with charging by conduction (direct contact transfers charge of the same sign) and triboelectric (rubbing materials separates charges).

Excess charge on a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium resides ENTIRELY on the surface, distributed so that interior E = 0 and the surface is equipotential. Higher surface charge density at points of higher curvature (sharper points concentrate charge) — explains corona discharge from lightning rods. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic: a charged conductor's interior has zero E and zero excess charge density (Gauss's law applied to a Gaussian surface just inside the conductor surface). The surface charge density adjusts itself to maintain these conditions. Lightning rod design (Franklin, 1752) uses concentrated curvature to bleed charge to atmosphere safely.

Faraday cage: a hollow conductor (closed mesh or solid) that shields its interior from external electric fields. Charges on the cage rearrange to cancel any external E inside — interior remains at E = 0. Michael Faraday demonstrated this with his 'ice-pail experiment' (1843). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) explain why cars are relatively safe in thunderstorms (metal frame acts as a Faraday cage), why MRI machines have shielded rooms (block external RF noise), and why airplane passengers survive lightning strikes (skin currents flow around the aluminum fuselage, not through the cabin). Even imperfect cages (wire mesh) work effectively if mesh opening << external-field wavelength.

questions.prompt: Law relating flux to enclosed charge.
questions.explanation: Gauss's law: the total electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by epsilon_0. Phi_E = integral E · dA = Q_enc / epsilon_0 (V*m). Carl Friedrich Gauss formulated this. One of Maxwell's four equations (in differential form). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) leverage Gauss's law to compute E for highly symmetric charge distributions (spherical, cylindrical, planar) where direct Coulomb integration is hard. Pick a Gaussian surface matching the symmetry, evaluate flux easily, equate to Q_enc / epsilon_0. Vacuum permittivity: epsilon_0 = 8.85 * 10^-12 C^2/(N*m^2). Equivalent to Coulomb's law combined with superposition.

choices: Flux equals enclosed charge over epsilon nought
questions.correctText: Flux equals enclosed charge over epsilon nought
questions.explanation: Phi_E = Q_enc / epsilon_0 (V*m) — flux through any closed surface equals enclosed charge over vacuum permittivity. On the AP equation sheet. Q_enc is total NET enclosed charge (sum of positive and negative within the Gaussian surface). epsilon_0 = 8.85 * 10^-12 C^2/(N*m^2). Independent of surface shape, surface size, or location of charges within — only enclosed charge matters. Charges OUTSIDE the surface contribute zero net flux (same field lines enter and exit). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test conceptual flux problems: a 5 C charge inside a Gaussian sphere of radius 1 m, then 2 m, gives the same flux 5/epsilon_0 — flux depends only on enclosed charge, not surface size.

choices: Flux equals enclosed charge over epsilon nought
questions.explanation: Spherical Gaussian surface — chosen when source charge has spherical symmetry (point charge, charged solid sphere, charged spherical shell). On a sphere of radius r centered on the symmetric charge, E is radial and constant in magnitude: Phi = E * 4 pi r^2 = Q_enc / epsilon_0 → E = Q_enc / (4 pi epsilon_0 r^2) = k Q_enc / r^2 (recovering Coulomb's law). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) classic: charged solid sphere of radius R — for r > R, E acts as if all charge were at center; for r < R, only enclosed fraction contributes (Q_enc = Q (r/R)^3 for uniform density), giving E = kQr/R^3 (linear in r inside). Shell theorem: hollow shell has E = 0 inside, kQ/r^2 outside.

Cylindrical Gaussian surface — chosen when source charge has cylindrical symmetry (infinite line charge, infinite charged rod, coaxial cable). For an infinite line charge with linear charge density lambda (C/m), choose a cylinder of radius r and length L centered on the line. Flux through curved side: Phi = E * 2 pi r L (top and bottom flux are zero — E perpendicular to side); enclosed charge: Q_enc = lambda L. Gauss's law: E = lambda / (2 pi epsilon_0 r). E falls as 1/r (not 1/r^2 — slower decay than point charge due to extended geometry). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) classic for coaxial cables and long wires.

choices: Flux equals enclosed charge over epsilon nought
questions.explanation: Gaussian pillbox: a small flat 'box' or cylinder with axis perpendicular to a charged plane, used when source has planar symmetry (infinite charged sheet, capacitor plate). For infinite sheet with surface charge density sigma (C/m^2), choose a pillbox of area A piercing the sheet. Flux through both faces: Phi = 2 E A (symmetric on both sides); enclosed charge: Q_enc = sigma A. Gauss: E = sigma / (2 epsilon_0). E is uniform on each side — independent of distance from sheet (a remarkable result of planar symmetry). For a CONDUCTOR'S surface, E just outside = sigma/epsilon_0 (factor of 2 different from isolated sheet). AP FRQ classic for parallel-plate capacitors.

Ohm's Law: V = I R (volts), with V the voltage drop across a resistor (V), I the current (A), R the resistance (Ohm). Georg Ohm (1827) discovered the linear relation in metal wires. On the AP equation sheet. Strictly valid for 'ohmic' materials (metals, most resistors); non-ohmic materials (diodes, light bulbs at varying T) have nonlinear V(I) curves. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Ohm's law with Kirchhoff's rules in multi-loop circuits, with power formulas (P = IV = I^2 R = V^2/R), and with capacitor charging dynamics. Microscopic origin: drift velocity of conduction electrons proportional to applied E.

Ohm (Ω) = 1 V/A — SI unit of electrical resistance. Named after Georg Simon Ohm (1789-1854). 1 Ohm: a conductor through which 1 A flows when 1 V is applied. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: V (volts) / I (amps) = R (ohms). Practical magnitudes: a copper wire 1 m long, 1 mm^2 cross-section: ~0.017 Ohm; AAA battery internal resistance ~0.2 Ohm; typical resistor in AP problems 10-10,000 Ohm; insulators > 10^10 Ohm; superconductors exactly 0 Ohm below critical T. Resistance depends on geometry (R = rho L/A) and material resistivity rho (Ohm*m).

Ampere (A) = 1 C/s — SI unit of electric current. Named after André-Marie Ampère (1775-1836), pioneer of electrodynamics. 1 A: 1 coulomb of charge flowing per second past a point. Defined in 2019 SI revision in terms of elementary charge: 1 A = (1/1.602 * 10^-19) e/s ≈ 6.24 * 10^18 electrons per second. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency in I = dq/dt or I = q/t (for steady current). Practical magnitudes: AAA battery ~0.05 A in a small device; phone charger ~1 A; household circuit breaker 15-20 A; car starter motor ~200 A; lightning bolt ~10^4 A peak (50,000 A possible).

Electric current I = dq/dt (amperes), the rate of charge flow past a point. Conventional current flows in the direction positive charges would flow — OPPOSITE to actual electron drift in metals (electrons are negative). Defined by Benjamin Franklin (1751) before electron discovery. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require careful sign and direction tracking. Microscopic relation: I = n A v_d q, where n is carrier density, A cross-section, v_d drift velocity (~10^-4 m/s for typical wire — much slower than signal-propagation speed ~c, which is the rate of field changes, not particle motion).

Power dissipated by a resistor: P = V^2 / R (watts), equivalently P = I^2 R = V * I. On the AP equation sheet. Derived from W = q V and P = dW/dt = (dq/dt) V = I V; substituting V = IR or I = V/R gives the alternate forms. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test selection of correct form: V^2/R when V across resistor is known; I^2 R when current through it is known; IV otherwise. Heat dissipation in resistors (joule heating, after James Joule 1841): the lost electrical energy becomes thermal energy. Used in heaters, incandescent bulbs, electrical fuses. Higher V at same R dissipates QUADRATICALLY more power.

Series connection: components arranged end-to-end so the same current flows through each. Resistors in series: R_total = R_1 + R_2 + ... + R_n. Voltage drops add: V_total = V_1 + V_2 + ... (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law). Current is uniform throughout. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test series circuits with: voltage divider rule V_R = V_total * R/R_total; total resistance increases when more resistors added; if one resistor opens (R = infinity), entire circuit current goes to zero (Christmas tree light failure mode). Series capacitors: 1/C_total = sum(1/C_i) — same form as parallel resistors.

choices: V_R equals V times R over R_total
questions.explanation: Series resistance: R_eq = R_1 + R_2 + ... + R_n (Ohm). Resistances add directly because the same current flows through each component, and the voltage drops add (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law). On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in voltage-divider problems: V across each resistor = V_total * (R_i / R_total). Limiting cases (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): adding a resistor in series ALWAYS increases total resistance; equal series resistors split voltage equally. For 3 series resistors of 10, 20, 30 Ohm: R_eq = 60 Ohm; with 12 V battery, I = 0.2 A; voltage drops are 2, 4, 6 V.

choices: V_R equals V times R over R_total
questions.explanation: Current is the SAME at every point in a series circuit (no junctions for charge to accumulate or split). Consequence of conservation of charge applied to the simple loop. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this conceptually: students must explain why I_1 = I_2 = I_n through all resistors in series, and why V drops across each are proportional to R (since V = IR and I is uniform). Loop current method (Kirchhoff): write a single equation V_battery = I R_total for series, solve for I. Practical: Christmas tree lights in series — if one bulb dies (open circuit), whole string goes out (modern strings use parallel sets to avoid this).

choices: V_R equals V times R over R_total
questions.explanation: Voltage drops in a series circuit add to the source EMF: V_source = V_1 + V_2 + ... + V_n. This is Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL, 1845): sum of voltage changes around any closed loop is zero. Energy conservation perspective: as a unit charge traverses the loop, the EMF source pumps it up by V_source J/C, and resistors drop it down by V_1 + V_2 + ... J/C — they must balance. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test this in multi-loop circuits where KVL is applied loop by loop. Voltage divider rule: V across each R = V_total * R / R_total.

choices: V_R equals V times R over R_total
questions.correctText: V_R equals V times R over R_total
questions.explanation: Voltage divider rule: V_i = V_source * R_i / R_total — voltage across each series resistor is proportional to its share of the total resistance. Derived from V_i = I R_i with I = V_source / R_total. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): in a series circuit with 9 V battery and resistors 30, 60 Ohm, V_30 = 9 * 30/90 = 3 V; V_60 = 9 * 60/90 = 6 V (notice the 1:2 ratio matches the resistance ratio). Used in electronic engineering for reference voltages, sensor biasing, and DAC ladders. Loaded voltage dividers require careful analysis: drawing significant current through R_load alters V_out (Thevenin equivalent).

choices: R_1 R_2 over R_1 plus R_2
questions.explanation: Parallel connection: components share both endpoints, providing multiple paths for current. Resistors in parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + ... + 1/R_n. Voltage is the same across each branch (KVL applied to loops sharing both nodes). Currents through branches add: I_total = I_1 + I_2 + ... (Kirchhoff's Current Law). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test parallel: R_eq is always less than the smallest individual R (more paths → less resistance). For two equal resistors in parallel, R_eq = R/2. Parallel capacitors: C_total = sum(C_i) — opposite to series resistors. Used in household wiring.

choices: n_1 sin theta_1 equals n_2 sin theta_2
questions.explanation: Parallel resistance: 1/R_eq = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + ... + 1/R_n. Reciprocals add. On the AP equation sheet. For two resistors: R_eq = R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: 10 Ohm and 30 Ohm in parallel give R_eq = (10*30)/(40) = 7.5 Ohm — LESS than the smallest individual. Each branch operates independently with V_source across it. Limiting cases (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): adding a resistor in parallel ALWAYS decreases total resistance; equal parallel resistors share current equally. R_eq for n identical R in parallel = R/n.

choices: R_1 R_2 over R_1 plus R_2
questions.explanation: Voltage is the SAME across each parallel branch — equal to the source EMF (for a simple parallel network). Consequence of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the loop containing each branch and the source. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this conceptually: students must explain why V_1 = V_2 = ... = V_source for parallel resistors, and why I through each is proportional to 1/R (since I = V/R and V is uniform). Practical: household wiring places appliances in PARALLEL across the 120 V line — each appliance sees 120 V regardless of how many others are running, and turning one off doesn't affect others.

choices: R_1 R_2 over R_1 plus R_2
questions.explanation: Currents through parallel branches add: I_total = I_1 + I_2 + ... + I_n. Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL, 1845): sum of currents into a junction equals sum out — conservation of charge. Each branch current: I_i = V / R_i (smaller R takes larger I). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: 10 Ohm and 30 Ohm in parallel across 12 V: I_10 = 1.2 A, I_30 = 0.4 A, I_total = 1.6 A. Check: I_total * R_eq = 1.6 * 7.5 = 12 V ✓. Current divider rule: I_i = I_total * R_other / (R_i + R_other) for two resistors — the larger R carries the smaller fraction of total I.

choices: R_1 R_2 over R_1 plus R_2
questions.correctText: R_1 R_2 over R_1 plus R_2
questions.explanation: Two parallel resistors: R_eq = R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2). Algebraic shortcut for the 'product over sum' rule, derived from 1/R_eq = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2 = (R_1 + R_2)/(R_1 R_2). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): 6 Ohm and 12 Ohm in parallel: R_eq = (6*12)/(18) = 4 Ohm. Limiting cases: if R_2 >> R_1, then R_eq ≈ R_1 (the smaller resistor dominates); if R_1 = R_2, then R_eq = R/2. Useful for circuit reduction — simplify two-by-two until only one equivalent resistor remains, then use V = I R_eq to find total current.

questions.prompt: Sum of currents at junction equals zero.
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL, also called the Junction Rule): at any junction in a circuit, the sum of currents IN equals the sum of currents OUT — equivalently, the algebraic sum (with sign convention) equals zero. Gustav Kirchhoff (1845). Expresses conservation of charge: charge cannot accumulate at a steady-state junction. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply KCL to find unknown currents in multi-loop circuits: at a junction with I_1 entering, I_2 and I_3 leaving → I_1 = I_2 + I_3. Common AP setup: KCL combined with KVL gives a system of equations to solve.

questions.prompt: Sum of voltages around loop equals zero.
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL, also Loop Rule): sum of voltage changes around any closed circuit loop equals zero. Gustav Kirchhoff (1845). Expresses conservation of energy: a unit charge returning to start has zero net energy change. EMF sources contribute +V (energy added by source); resistors contribute -IR (energy dropped to heat); capacitors contribute -Q/C. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) traverse each loop systematically, applying sign conventions consistently (e.g., entering a battery from -, gain V; entering from +, lose V). Combined with KCL yields a solvable system for multi-loop circuits.

Gustav Robert Kirchhoff (1824-1887), German physicist. Formulated his circuit laws (KCL, KVL) in 1845 at age 21. Also major contributor to spectroscopy (with Bunsen — Bunsen burner!), discovering with Bunsen that each element has a characteristic emission/absorption spectrum (Kirchhoff-Bunsen lines, 1859). Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation (1859): emissivity = absorptivity at thermal equilibrium — foundation of blackbody radiation theory, later quantized by Planck (1900). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test KCL/KVL conceptually: students must justify each rule via conservation of charge (KCL) and energy (KVL) before invoking it.

questions.prompt: Method using loop and junction rules.
questions.explanation: Circuit analysis using Kirchhoff's laws: (1) Identify junctions; (2) Identify independent loops; (3) Assign current directions (guess — if you guess wrong, the answer will come out negative); (4) Apply KCL at each junction (n-1 equations for n junctions); (5) Apply KVL around each loop (one equation per loop); (6) Solve the linear system for unknown currents and voltages. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test 2- and 3-loop circuits with multiple batteries: students must set up equations carefully and solve simultaneously. Modern PSpice/LTSpice software automates this — but AP-level expects manual analysis.

questions.prompt: Loop rule based on conservation of this.
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law is a statement of energy conservation in circuits: a unit positive charge traversing a closed loop returns to its starting potential, so the algebraic sum of voltage gains (from EMF sources) equals the sum of voltage drops (across resistors, capacitors, inductors). If KVL were violated, the charge would gain or lose energy each loop — perpetual motion. KCL similarly expresses charge conservation. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require explicit invocation of conservation laws when justifying Kirchhoff's rules in paragraph-length arguments — graders look for the words 'conservation of energy' (KVL) and 'conservation of charge' (KCL).

Capacitor: a device storing electric charge and energy in an electric field between two conductive plates separated by an insulator (dielectric). Charge stored: Q = C V (coulombs). Invented as the Leyden jar (1745, Cunaeus and von Kleist). Modern capacitors range from picofarads (radio tuning) to farads (super-capacitors for energy storage). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test capacitor energy U = (1/2) C V^2 = (1/2) Q V = (1/2) Q^2/C and RC time constants (tau = R C for charging/discharging). Parallel-plate capacitance: C = epsilon_0 A / d, with epsilon_0 = 8.85 * 10^-12 C^2/(N*m^2). Dielectric kappa multiplier increases C by factor kappa.

Capacitance C = Q / V (farads = C/V) — charge stored per unit voltage across the plates. Property of capacitor geometry and dielectric material, NOT of the applied voltage or charge. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: a 10 μF capacitor charged to 9 V holds Q = 90 μC. For parallel-plate: C = kappa epsilon_0 A / d (F), with kappa the dielectric constant (kappa = 1 vacuum/air, ~3 paper, ~5 mica, ~80 water, ~7000 PZT ceramic). Increasing A or kappa raises C; increasing d lowers C. Energy stored: U = (1/2) C V^2 = (1/2) Q V = (1/2) Q^2/C. Used in oscillators, filters, energy storage.

Farad (F) = 1 C/V — SI unit of capacitance. Named after Michael Faraday (1791-1867), pioneer of electromagnetism. 1 F is enormous; typical AP-level capacitors are μF (10^-6 F) or pF (10^-12 F). A 1 F capacitor charged to 1 V stores 1 C — a huge amount of charge (>10^18 electrons). Super-capacitors (electric double-layer): up to thousands of farads but at low voltages. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: C in F, V in V → Q in C and U = (1/2) C V^2 in J. Faraday's contributions: laws of electrolysis (1834), electromagnetic induction (1831), Faraday cage.

Energy stored in a capacitor: U_C = (1/2) C V^2 = (1/2) Q V = (1/2) Q^2/C (joules). Derived by integrating dW = V dq = (q/C) dq from 0 to Q: U = Q^2/(2C). On the AP equation sheet. Factor 1/2 because voltage rises linearly with charge during charging — energy stored is half what you'd get if V were constant at final value. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain with energy conservation: a 100 μF capacitor charged to 12 V stores U = (1/2)(10^-4)(144) = 0.0072 J = 7.2 mJ. Camera flashes store ~50 J; defibrillator capacitors ~360 J at 5 kV; super-capacitors enable regenerative braking in electric vehicles.

Parallel-plate capacitor: C = epsilon_0 A / d (farads) for vacuum/air dielectric, with A the plate area (m^2), d separation (m), epsilon_0 = 8.85 * 10^-12 C^2/(N*m^2). With dielectric: C = kappa epsilon_0 A / d, where kappa is the dimensionless dielectric constant (water 80, mica 5, paper 3, PZT 7000). On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): plates of area 100 cm^2 = 0.01 m^2, separation 1 mm = 0.001 m, vacuum: C = (8.85e-12)(0.01)/(0.001) ≈ 89 pF. Increase d → less C (electrons feel each other less across plates). Increase A → more C. Add dielectric → reduces internal E by kappa, allowing more charge for same V.

questions.prompt: Force on moving charge in B field.
questions.explanation: Lorentz force on a moving charge: F = q v x B (newtons), with v velocity (m/s) and B magnetic field (T). Magnitude F = q v B sin(theta), where theta is the angle between v and B. Direction by right-hand rule (for positive charge). Hendrik Lorentz (1853-1928) formalized this in 1895; the full Lorentz force law F = q(E + v x B) underlies all electromagnetism. On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use it for cyclotron motion (charged particle in uniform B follows a circle of radius r = mv/(qB)), velocity selector (E and B perpendicular cancel for v = E/B), and mass spectrometer.

Magnetic force magnitude on a moving charge: F = q v B sin(theta), where q is charge (C), v speed (m/s), B magnetic field (T), theta angle between v and B. On the AP equation sheet. Maximum F when v perpendicular to B (sin 90 = 1); zero F when v parallel to B (sin 0 = 0). Direction by right-hand rule. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: a proton moving at 10^6 m/s perpendicular to B = 0.1 T experiences F = (1.6e-19)(10^6)(0.1) = 1.6 * 10^-14 N — tiny but creates circular motion. Circular motion: q v B = m v^2 / r → r = mv/(qB), and period T = 2 pi m / (q B) (independent of speed!).

Tesla (T) = 1 N/(A*m) = 1 kg/(A*s^2) = 1 Wb/m^2 — SI unit of magnetic field B. Named after Nikola Tesla (1856-1943), inventor and electrical engineer. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: q in C, v in m/s, B in T → F in N (matches Lorentz formula). Practical magnitudes: Earth's surface B ≈ 5 * 10^-5 T (50 μT); refrigerator magnet ~10^-2 T; typical lab electromagnet 0.1-1 T; MRI machine 1.5-7 T; world-record sustained 45 T (NHMFL); neutron star surfaces 10^8-10^11 T. Older unit: gauss (G); 1 T = 10^4 G.

Force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field: F = B I L sin(theta) (N), where B is field (T), I current (A), L wire length in the field (m), theta angle between current direction and B. On the AP equation sheet. Derived from Lorentz force summed over all moving charge carriers in the wire: F = (n A L) (q v_d) B sin(theta) = I L B sin(theta). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: 0.5 m wire carrying 2 A perpendicular to B = 0.1 T experiences F = (0.1)(2)(0.5) = 0.1 N. Underlies all electric motors: torque on a current loop in B produces rotation, with brushes/commutators reversing I to maintain unidirectional torque.

questions.prompt: Direction of magnetic force on charge.
questions.explanation: Right-hand rule for magnetic force F = q v x B: for positive charge, point fingers along v, curl toward B; thumb gives F direction. For negative charge, F points opposite. Equivalent: use the slap-style RHR — fingers in v direction, curl toward B, thumb gives F (for positive q). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require explicit RHR application on every magnetic-force problem. For a positive charge moving east in a B field pointing north, F = qv x B points UP. Cyclotron motion: F always perpendicular to v → centripetal → circular motion with r = mv/(qB). Used in particle accelerators, mass spectrometers, MRI.

Magnetic field of long straight wire: B = mu_0 I / (2 pi r) (T), at perpendicular distance r from the wire carrying current I. Direction by right-hand rule: thumb along I, fingers curl in B direction (circles around wire). mu_0 = 4 pi * 10^-7 T*m/A is vacuum permeability. On the AP equation sheet. Ampère and Biot-Savart developed this in the 1820s. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: 10 A current in a wire, B at r = 1 cm = (4 pi * 10^-7)(10)/(2 pi * 0.01) = 2 * 10^-4 T = 0.2 mT. Falls as 1/r (not 1/r^2 — slower decay than point charges). Used in transmission-line field calculations.

Magnetic field inside a long solenoid: B = mu_0 n I (T), where n is turns per unit length (turns/m) and I is current (A). Uniform along the solenoid axis, approximately zero outside (for an ideal infinite solenoid). On the AP equation sheet. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: a solenoid with 1000 turns/m carrying 5 A produces B = (4 pi * 10^-7)(1000)(5) ≈ 6.3 mT inside. Used in MRI machines, particle accelerators, electromagnets, relays. Note: B is independent of solenoid radius (for ideal infinite case) — only depends on turns per length and current. Derived from Ampère's law applied to a rectangular Amperian loop.

Solenoid parameter n = N/L (turns per meter), with N total turns and L solenoid length (m). The B = mu_0 n I formula shows B is determined by current density (Amp-turns per meter), not by absolute current alone. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test: doubling turns per length doubles B at same current; doubling current at same n doubles B. Densely wound solenoids (high n) produce strong fields per amp of current. Typical AP-level solenoid: ~1000-10,000 turns/m carrying ~1-10 A produces ~1-100 mT. MRI machines achieve 1.5-7 T using superconducting solenoids (very high n, very high I).

choices: d sine theta equals m lambda
questions.explanation: Permanent magnets' B field arises from aligned atomic magnetic moments — each electron has an intrinsic magnetic moment from its spin. In ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt), exchange interactions cause neighboring electron spins to align within microscopic 'domains'. Heating above the Curie temperature (770 °C for iron) randomizes domains, destroying magnetization. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) explain qualitatively: students must distinguish permanent magnets (intrinsic alignment) from electromagnets (current-induced fields). Modern rare-earth magnets (NdFeB) achieve ~1.4 T surface field using exotic crystal structures. Magnetic monopoles have never been observed — Maxwell's div B = 0.

Magnetic field at the center of a circular current loop of radius R: B = mu_0 I / (2 R) (T). On the AP equation sheet. Direction along the loop axis (right-hand rule: curl fingers along I, thumb gives B). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: a 0.1 m radius loop carrying 5 A produces B = (4 pi * 10^-7)(5)/(0.2) ≈ 3.1 * 10^-5 T at center. For N stacked loops: B = mu_0 N I / (2 R). Derived from Biot-Savart law integrated over the loop. The loop's magnetic moment: mu = I A = I pi R^2 (A*m^2). Used in Helmholtz coils (two coaxial loops separated by R) to produce uniform B in a central region — laboratory standard.

questions.prompt: EMF induced by changing magnetic flux.
questions.explanation: Faraday's law of induction (Michael Faraday, 1831): a time-changing magnetic flux through a circuit induces an EMF: EMF = -d(Phi_B)/dt (volts), where Phi_B = integral B · dA is magnetic flux (T*m^2 = Wb). On the AP equation sheet. One of Maxwell's four equations. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Faraday's law with: moving conductor (motional EMF = BLv); time-changing B (transformer EMF); changing area (loop expanding into a field). Direction of induced current: Lenz's law (opposes the flux change). Foundation of generators, transformers, induction stoves, electric guitar pickups, MRI gradient coils.

choices: EMF equals minus d Phi over dt
questions.correctText: EMF equals minus d Phi over dt
questions.explanation: EMF = -d(Phi_B)/dt (volts) — Faraday's law. Phi_B = B A cos(theta) for uniform B (T*m^2 = Wb). On the AP equation sheet. Negative sign embodies Lenz's law (induced EMF opposes flux change — energy conservation). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) handle three causes of flux change: (1) B changes (transformer EMF), (2) A changes (sliding-rod or expanding-loop), (3) theta changes (rotating loop = generator). For N-turn coil: EMF = -N d(Phi)/dt. Used in induction cooktops (oscillating B induces eddy currents in iron pan, heating via I^2 R), wireless phone chargers, electric guitars, electromagnetic brakes.

questions.prompt: Direction of induced current opposes change.
questions.explanation: Lenz's law (Heinrich Lenz, 1834): the direction of induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it. Consequence of conservation of energy — if current FLOWED to assist the change, energy would multiply without input (perpetual motion). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require students to apply Lenz's law in paragraph-length arguments: a magnet approaching a loop induces current creating a B field that REPELS the magnet (must do mechanical work to push magnet in — that work becomes the electrical energy in the loop). Lenz's law is the qualitative side of Faraday's quantitative law (the negative sign in EMF = -dPhi/dt).

choices: EMF equals minus d Phi over dt
questions.explanation: Lenz's law is required by conservation of energy: if the induced current's B field REINFORCED the flux change instead of opposing it, then the magnet would accelerate spontaneously toward the loop, gaining KE while also generating electrical energy in the loop — both energies free, violating conservation. Lenz's opposition forces external work to be done against the induced opposing force, providing the energy delivered to the circuit. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) demand explicit invocation of energy conservation when justifying Lenz's law in paragraph-length arguments — graders look for 'conservation of energy' as the underlying reason for the negative sign.

choices: EMF equals minus d Phi over dt
n_1 sin theta_1 equals n_2 sin theta_2
questions.explanation: Electric generator: a rotating coil in a magnetic field (or vice versa) that produces EMF via Faraday's law. As the coil rotates with angular velocity omega, Phi = B A cos(omega t), giving EMF = -d(Phi)/dt = B A omega sin(omega t) — sinusoidal alternating voltage of amplitude B A omega. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: a 100-turn coil of area 0.05 m^2 in B = 0.5 T rotating at 60 Hz produces peak EMF = 100 * 0.5 * 0.05 * 2 pi * 60 ≈ 942 V. Hydroelectric, coal, nuclear, wind, and gas turbines all convert mechanical energy to electrical via generators. Faraday built the first homopolar generator in 1831.

Self-inductance L (henries) — the property of a coil to oppose changes in its own current via self-induced EMF: EMF = -L (dI/dt) (V). For a solenoid: L = mu_0 N^2 A / l, with N total turns, A cross-section, l length. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test how inductors resist current changes: in an RL circuit, current builds up exponentially with time constant tau = L/R; when source is removed, current decays exponentially. Inductors store magnetic energy U_L = (1/2) L I^2 (J). Applications: filter chokes (smooth ripple in DC supplies), tuned LC circuits (radio), transformers (mutual inductance).

Henry (H) = 1 V*s/A = 1 Wb/A — SI unit of inductance. Named after Joseph Henry (1797-1878), American physicist who independently discovered electromagnetic induction (slightly before Faraday's 1831 announcement). 1 H: a coil where 1 A/s rate of current change induces 1 V opposing EMF. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency in EMF = -L dI/dt formulas. Practical magnitudes: small AP-level inductor ~1 mH (10^-3 H); large radio-tuning coil ~1 μH; MRI gradient coils ~mH; massive AC-line filter chokes ~10s of H. RL time constant tau = L/R (seconds).

Energy stored in inductor: U_L = (1/2) L I^2 (joules), with L inductance (H) and I current (A). On the AP equation sheet. Stored in the magnetic field within and around the inductor's coils. Symmetric to capacitor energy U_C = (1/2) C V^2 — both quadratic in their respective storage variables. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain U_L with LC oscillation: in an ideal LC tank circuit, energy oscillates between U_L = (1/2) L I^2 and U_C = (1/2) C V^2 at frequency f = 1/(2 pi sqrt(LC)). Used in flyback transformers (TV CRTs), spark coils, superconducting magnetic energy storage (SMES).

Self-inductance L is the property of a circuit element (typically a coil) to oppose changes in its OWN current. EMF_self = -L (dI/dt) — Lenz-law consequence applied to self-flux. AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test conceptually: when a switch closes in an RL circuit, current does NOT jump instantly to V/R — it grows exponentially: I(t) = (V/R)(1 - exp(-t R/L)), reaching ~63% of final value at t = tau = L/R. When the switch opens, the inductor's back-EMF can produce a huge voltage spike (sparking across contacts) — explains the snap on car ignition coils, where a sharp dI/dt creates ~10^4 V across spark plug gaps.

RL circuit time constant: tau = L/R (seconds). Determines how quickly current builds up (when switch closes) or decays (when switch opens) in an inductor-resistor circuit. Current build-up: I(t) = (V/R)(1 - exp(-t/tau)) — reaches ~63% of final value at t = tau, ~99% at t = 5 tau. Symmetric to RC time constant tau_RC = R C (units of seconds in both). AP Physics 2 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: 10 H inductor with 100 Ohm resistor: tau = 0.1 s; current reaches 63% of V/R in 0.1 s. Inductors 'fight' rapid current changes — used to smooth power-supply ripple, prevent contact arcing, and as electromagnetic shock absorbers.

choices: V_s over V_p equals N_s over N_p
d sine theta equals m lambda
questions.explanation: Transformer: two coils (primary and secondary) magnetically coupled, often around a shared iron core, used to step up or step down AC voltage by Faraday's law. AC current in primary creates oscillating B, which couples into the secondary and induces EMF there. Faraday and Henry independently invented the principle (1831).
